mainEntity.name: What is the Farnsworth-Munsell 100 Hue Test?

acceptedAnswer.text: It is a recognised assessment of hue discrimination in which you arrange coloured tiles into a smooth sequence. A lower score indicates better colour perception.

mainEntity.name: Why does colour perception matter for photographers?

acceptedAnswer.text: Editing, colour grading and printing rely on accurate hue recognition. Weak hue perception can introduce subtle casts that look unnatural in skin tones, foliage and prints.

mainEntity.name: How do I read my score?

acceptedAnswer.text: 0–20 = excellent; 21–50 = good; 51–100 = moderate; 100+ = limited. Higher scores benefit from neutral lighting, reference targets and second-pair-of-eyes checks.

mainEntity.name: Do I need special equipment to take the test?

acceptedAnswer.text: Use a desktop or laptop with a mouse. Mobile devices are not supported. View in neutral light and avoid screen night-shift modes to prevent colour bias.

mainEntity.name: Can I compensate for weaker colour perception?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes. Use a grey card at capture, edit under neutral 5000–6500K lighting, calibrate your monitor, make small A/B edits, use reference images and seek feedback on colour-critical work.
